questions.question: What does decorating a controller with `[ApiController]` give you out of the box?
options: Auto-400 on invalid `ModelState`, binding-source inference, `ProblemDetails` errors, and required attribute routing
Automatic JSON-to-XML content negotiation plus a bundled, ready-to-use Swagger UI page
Automatic API versioning wired across URL, header, and media-type version readers
Automatic rate-limiting and CORS policies applied uniformly to every action method
questions.explanation: `[ApiController]` enables auto-400 on invalid ModelState, binding inference (body for complex types, route/query for simple), ProblemDetails error bodies, and requires attribute routing. Versioning, rate limiting, CORS, and a Swagger UI are all separate opt-in concerns, not part of `[ApiController]`.

questions.question: By default, how does ASP.NET Core's default serializer (System.Text.Json) treat property names and fields when writing JSON?
options: camelCase property names, and it does not serialize fields by default
PascalCase property names, and it serializes public fields by default
snake_case property names, and it serializes every member including private fields
camelCase property names, and it serializes public fields by default
questions.explanation: STJ defaults to camelCase property names, case-insensitive reads, UTF-8, and does not serialize fields by default. PascalCase, snake_case, and field serialization are not the defaults.

questions.question: An action or endpoint can bind at most one parameter from the request body (`[FromBody]`).
questions.explanation: There can be at most one `[FromBody]` binding per action/endpoint, because the request body stream is read once. Multiple body-bound parameters are not supported.

questions.question: What is the purpose of returning `ActionResult<T>` from a controller action?
options: It lets the action return either the typed payload `T` or an alternative `IActionResult` such as `NotFound()`
It forces the action to always return the typed payload `T` and never a status-only result
It serializes `T` to XML by default instead of the usual JSON output
It defers serialization until after middleware runs so the result can be cached automatically
questions.explanation: `ActionResult<T>` uses implicit conversions so an action can return either the value `T` or a result like `NotFound()`. It does not force a payload, change the serializer, or add caching.

questions.question: Which Minimal API feature gives endpoints the shared route-prefix and shared-metadata grouping that controllers get for free?
options: `MapGroup`
`[AsParameters]`
`TypedResults`
`AddEndpointFilter`
questions.explanation: `MapGroup` creates a route group with a shared prefix and lets you attach metadata, auth, filters, and CORS to all endpoints in it, and groups compose. `[AsParameters]` bundles binding sources, `TypedResults` produces results, and `AddEndpointFilter` adds an interception layer.

questions.question: Given the endpoint below, what value does `id` bind to for the request `GET /items/5?id=99`?
options: 5 — the route value wins over the query string for an unattributed simple parameter
99 — the query string wins over the route value for simple parameters
The request fails with a 400 because `id` is ambiguous between the two sources
0 — neither source binds because no `[From...]` attribute is present on the parameter
questions.explanation: For an unattributed simple parameter, route values take precedence over the query string, so `id` binds to 5. The query value 99 is ignored; there is no ambiguity error and binding still occurs without an explicit attribute.

questions.question: Why prefer `TypedResults` over `Results` in Minimal APIs?
options: It returns strongly-typed `IResult` implementations, so endpoints are testable without casting and OpenAPI infers response types
It is significantly faster at runtime because it skips the JSON serialization step entirely
It automatically wraps every endpoint response in a standardized `ProblemDetails` envelope
`Results` was deprecated and removed in .NET 10, leaving `TypedResults` as the only choice
questions.explanation: `TypedResults` returns concrete `IResult` types, giving compile-time type info that tests can assert on and OpenAPI can read; runtime behavior is the same as `Results`. It is not faster, does not force ProblemDetails, and `Results` is not removed.

questions.question: Given this controller, what happens for a POST with an invalid model (e.g. missing the required `Name`)?
options: A 400 with a `ValidationProblemDetails` body is returned and the action body is never reached
The action body runs and returns 200 with the partially-bound `dto` object
The action body runs but throws a `ValidationException` that you must catch
A 422 Unprocessable Entity is returned and the action body still runs afterward
questions.explanation: `[ApiController]` short-circuits invalid ModelState into a 400 `ValidationProblemDetails` before the action executes, so the body is never reached. It is a 400 (not 422), no exception is thrown, and the body does not run.

questions.question: Which standard governs the `ProblemDetails` error contract in modern ASP.NET Core, and how do validation failures differ?
options: RFC 9457 (obsoletes RFC 7807); validation failures use the `ValidationProblemDetails` subtype with an `errors` dictionary
RFC 7807 (still current); validation failures reuse the plain `ProblemDetails` shape with one `error` string
RFC 9457 (obsoletes RFC 7807); validation failures return a plain-text body instead of structured JSON
RFC 6585; validation failures are reported using a `violations` array of per-field objects
questions.explanation: ProblemDetails follows RFC 9457, which obsoletes RFC 7807, and validation errors use the `ValidationProblemDetails` subtype with an `errors` dictionary keyed by field. RFC 7807 is obsoleted, the body stays JSON (`application/problem+json`), and RFC 6585 is unrelated.

questions.question: On a fresh .NET 10 project, a teammate adds `builder.Services.AddSwaggerGen()` expecting Swagger UI at `/swagger`. What is the correct assessment?
options: Swashbuckle was dropped from the templates in .NET 9; the built-in `AddOpenApi()`/`MapOpenApi()` serves only the document, so a UI must be added separately
`AddSwaggerGen()` is the supported built-in API here; the only mistake is forgetting to also call `app.UseSwaggerUI()`
Swashbuckle still ships in the .NET 10 templates, so the Swagger UI appears automatically once `AddSwaggerGen` is registered
OpenAPI is not supported for Minimal APIs at all, so the team must switch the project to controllers first
questions.explanation: Swashbuckle was removed from the templates in .NET 9. The framework's built-in `Microsoft.AspNetCore.OpenApi` (`AddOpenApi`/`MapOpenApi`) generates the document for both controllers and Minimal APIs but ships no UI, so Scalar/Swagger UI/NSwag must be added separately.

questions.question: What does `app.MapOpenApi()` actually expose by default?
options: The OpenAPI document as JSON at `/openapi/v1.json` (YAML with a `.yaml`/`.yml` suffix) — not an interactive UI
An interactive Swagger UI page served automatically at the `/swagger` route
Both the JSON document and a fully bundled Scalar UI, enabled automatically
A redirect that sends the browser to the externally hosted Swagger Editor
questions.explanation: `MapOpenApi` serves the OpenAPI document (JSON at `/openapi/v1.json`, or YAML with a `.yaml`/`.yml` suffix). No interactive UI ships; you add Scalar or another UI separately.

questions.question: Before .NET 10, Minimal APIs had no built-in DataAnnotations validation — you had to validate manually or use a library such as FluentValidation.
questions.explanation: Built-in Minimal API validation arrived in .NET 10 via `AddValidation()` plus a source-generated interceptor. Before .NET 10 there was no built-in Minimal-API validation, so manual checks or a library were required.

questions.question: Which statements about middleware, MVC filters, and endpoint filters are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Middleware runs for the whole pipeline and is endpoint-agnostic — it knows nothing about the action/endpoint
MVC filters run inside MVC with access to `ModelState`, action arguments, and the result
Endpoint filters (`AddEndpointFilter`) are the Minimal API analog, wrapping the delegate with access to its arguments and result
Endpoint filters can only run before the delegate, never after it returns
Middleware has direct access to the bound action arguments and `ModelState`
questions.explanation: Middleware wraps the whole pipeline and is endpoint-agnostic; MVC filters run inside MVC with ModelState/argument/result access; endpoint filters wrap the Minimal API delegate with argument and result access. Endpoint filters run both before and after (they call `next` and can post-process), and middleware has no access to bound arguments or ModelState.

questions.question: A team configures STJ via `AddControllers().AddJsonOptions(...)` to emit enums as strings, but their Minimal API endpoints still serialize enums as numbers. What is the cause?
options: Minimal APIs use `Microsoft.AspNetCore.Http.Json.JsonOptions` (set via `ConfigureHttpJsonOptions`), separate from MVC's `JsonOptions`
Minimal APIs ignore all `JsonSerializerOptions` entirely and always fall back to the compile-time defaults
`AddJsonOptions` only ever applies to deserialization (reads), so it never affects serialized output
Enum-as-string needs source generation, which controllers turn on but Minimal APIs deliberately disable
questions.explanation: MVC's `JsonOptions` (set by `AddJsonOptions`) and Minimal APIs' `Http.Json.JsonOptions` (set by `ConfigureHttpJsonOptions`) are separate objects. Configuring one does not affect the other, which is why the Minimal API endpoints were unaffected.

questions.question: With only the default JSON formatter configured, a client sends `Accept: application/xml`. What does the server return by default, and how do you change it?
options: It falls back to JSON by default; set `ReturnHttpNotAcceptable = true` to return 406 Not Acceptable instead
It returns 406 Not Acceptable by default; set `ReturnHttpNotAcceptable = false` to fall back to JSON
It returns 415 Unsupported Media Type because the request's body content type is wrong
It throws an unhandled exception during formatting that surfaces to the client as a 500
questions.explanation: By default the server falls back to JSON when the requested type is unsupported. A 406 is only returned if you opt in with `ReturnHttpNotAcceptable = true`. 415 concerns the request body's Content-Type, not the Accept header.

questions.question: Why does this CORS configuration fail at runtime / get rejected by the browser?
options: `AllowAnyOrigin()` (wildcard `*`) cannot be combined with `AllowCredentials()`; use explicit `WithOrigins(...)` instead
`AllowCredentials()` is not a real method on the policy builder and does not even compile
A default policy is not allowed to call `AllowCredentials()` — only named policies can use credentials
`AllowAnyOrigin()` must always be paired with `AllowAnyMethod()` or the policy throws at startup
questions.explanation: A wildcard origin cannot be combined with credentials — browsers reject credentialed requests against `*`. You must specify explicit origins via `WithOrigins(...)` when `AllowCredentials()` is needed. The method exists and default policies may use it.

questions.question: Which statements about CORS and rate limiting wiring in ASP.NET Core are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: `UseCors` must sit before `UseAuthorization` and the endpoints it applies to
Rate limiting is built in since .NET 7 via `AddRateLimiter`/`UseRateLimiter`, with fixed window, sliding window, token bucket, and concurrency
Per-endpoint and per-group enforcement is attached with `RequireCors(...)` and `RequireRateLimiting("policy")`
Rate limiting requires a third-party NuGet package because it is not part of the framework
`AllowAnyOrigin()` and `AllowCredentials()` can be combined as long as `UseCors` runs last
questions.explanation: `UseCors` must precede authorization/endpoints; rate limiting has been built in since .NET 7 with those four algorithms; and `RequireCors`/`RequireRateLimiting` attach policies per endpoint or group. Rate limiting needs no third-party package, and wildcard origin can never be combined with credentials regardless of ordering.

questions.subtopic: DTOs vs entities at the boundary
questions.question: What is the primary risk, beyond schema leakage, of binding incoming requests directly to EF Core entities instead of input DTOs?
options: Over-posting (mass-assignment): a client can set entity properties the endpoint never meant to expose for writing
EF Core refuses to bind entities from the request body, so every such request fails with a 400
System.Text.Json cannot serialize entities at all, forcing the whole API to switch over to XML
Model binding silently runs database queries while binding, causing N+1 reads on each request
questions.explanation: Binding input to entities risks over-posting/mass-assignment, where a caller sets fields you never meant to be writable; it also couples the wire contract to the DB. Entities bind fine, STJ serializes them, and binding itself does not query the database.
